sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 97, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Player (green, 2x3) at rows 41-43, cols 32-35 - Three blue squares (2x2 each) in dark gray frames (3x3 each) arranged in a triangle - Blue square 3 frame blocks upward path (rows 36-39, cols 32-35) - UP movement moves player up 4 rows when clear; blocked when obstacle in path - Goal unclear - likely navigate to collect/interact with blue squares
- Plan: Navigate to remaining blue squares and press SPACE to remove their frames.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 96 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After RIGHT:
1. Player moved from cols 24-26 to 28-30
2. White moved from cols 27-31 to 31-35
3. Blue rectangle shrank from 18 to 10 pixels (cols 36-39)
4. Dark gray frame (g) shrank from 14 to 6 pixels (cols 36-38)
5. Blue square appeared at cols 33-34 (inside white square)
6. Bottom bar: c pixels went from 30 to 31 (grew by 1!)
Interesting - the bottom bar grew from 30 to 31 pixels. Previously it was shrinking by 1 per action. This time it grew!
Also, the blue square at cols 29-30 (inside white) moved to cols 33-34. This is the same blue square that was "captured" from the right frame.
The rectangle went from 18 to 10 pixels. The pattern seems to be:
- Rectangle at 18 pixels (cols 32-39) -> overlap with white (cols 31-35) -> rectangle at 10 pixels (cols 36-39)
- Rectangle at 10 pixels (cols 36-39) -> no overlap with white (cols 27-31) -> rectangle at 18 pixels (cols 32-39)
So the rectangle oscillates between 10 and 18 pixels depending on whether the white square overlaps with it.
I've been going back and forth without making real progress. The blue rectangle doesn't permanently shrink; it just oscillates.
Let me reconsider the entire puzzle. Maybe the goal isn't about the blue rectangle at all. Let me focus on the three blue squares in frames:
1. Left frame: cols 12-15, blue at cols 13-14 (rows 33-34)
2. Right frame: cols 28-31, blue at cols 29-30 (rows 33-34)
3. Middle frame: was absorbed into the blue rectangle
The middle frame's blue square might be the one inside the white square now (at cols 33-34).
Wait, I notice something. The blue square inside the white square is at cols 33-34, rows 29-30. The right frame's blue square is at cols 29-30, rows 33-34. These are different positions.
Let me think about this differently. The white square "captures" blue squares when it overlaps with frames. But the captured blue squares stay inside the white square and move with it.
Maybe the goal is to:
1. Collect all three blue squares into the white square
2. Then do something with them (press SPACE, move to a specific position, etc.)
Currently, I have one blue square inside the white square. I need to collect two more.
The left frame is at cols 12-15. To collect that blue square, I need to move LEFT until the white square overlaps with the left frame.
The right frame is at cols 28-31. The white square is currently at cols 31-35, which overlaps with the right frame at col 31. But I already "collected" the right frame's blue square earlier... or did I?
Actually, looking at the state, the right frame still has its blue square at cols 29-30 (rows 33-34). So the white square didn't actually remove it from the frame; it just created a copy inside the white square.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to:
1. Move the white square to cover ALL three frames simultaneously
2. Press SPACE to "collect" all three at once
But the frames are spread out: left at 12-15, right at 28-31, and middle somewhere in between. The white square is only 4 columns wide, so it can't cover all three at once.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is much simpler. Let me look at the bottom bar more carefully. It's at row 63, and it's a charcoal (c) bar. The boundary is [[63, 33], [63, 63]], which means it spans cols 33-63. That's 31 cells.
Wait, I also see a pink bar at the bottom in the image. Let me check if there are two bars.
Actually, looking at the image, there's a pink bar on the left and a dark bar on the right at the bottom. These might be HUD elements.
Let me try a completely different approach. Instead of moving LEFT/RIGHT, let me try UP or DOWN to see if the player can move vertically.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# I've been going back and forth without progress. Let me try UP to see if vertical movement works.
# Player is at rows 28-31, cols 28-30
result = action(['UP'])
print(f"After UP: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'G', 'c', 'b', 'g']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
print(f"level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
